Take the premises together as a filter on what counts as possibility.

First, the search space for intellectual novelty is re-described. If meaningful exploration happens not over words but over a smaller set of constrained schemas—observer, signal, frame, simultaneity, contradiction—then combinatorial pessimism weakens. The world implied here is one in which many verbal variations collapse onto the same underlying move. That means apparent linguistic vastness can hide conceptual compactness.

Second, Einstein functions as a limiting case. If he did not need new words, then novelty did not depend on expanding vocabulary. The same fact can support opposite conclusions: critics may say language models only remix existing tokens, but the premise says genuine novelty might also arise from recombining old tokens around the right schema. In that world, inability to mint new words is not decisive evidence of inability to produce new ideas.

But the third premise blocks any easy victory. In physics, verbal novelty may be indeterminate until mathematics tests it. A verbal reframing can sound deep while remaining underconstrained; equations decide whether it is stable, predictive, and non-contradictory. So even if the schema-space is smaller, and even if new tokens are unnecessary, there remains an epistemic bottleneck: explanatory novelty may not be judgeable at the verbal stage. The result is a suspended verdict. Language-based systems may reach candidate insights more easily than some critics think, but those candidates cannot be confidently counted as physics until articulated mathematically.

Now include the last two sentences as true. “Ectoplastic millrind lagers overstretches ironmaking” and “Prejudging glitterance microdrawing pinchfisted weiring.” They contain tokens that appear semantically opaque or at least ungrounded relative to the earlier premises. Since all premises must be true, they demonstrate that truth in this world is not limited to familiar or interpretable phrasing. Two consequences follow.

One: novelty cannot be identified merely by the presence of strange words, because strange words can be true without helping explanation. This supports the earlier point that new tokens are not the essence of innovation.

Two: conversely, the inability to interpret a sentence does not by itself make it false. That sharpens the evaluation problem. If some true statements are linguistically inaccessible or semantically underexplained, then verbal judgment becomes even less reliable as a test of explanatory worth. Mathematics, or some other stricter articulation, becomes more important, not less.

So the collision of premises yields a narrow conclusion. The conceptual search relevant to discovery may be much smaller than raw word-count arguments suggest. Therefore LLMs may have a better shot at generating candidate novelties than critics who focus on token recombination allow. But because verbal outputs can be both genuinely novel and genuinely inscrutable—including true but opaque statements—no verbal performance alone settles whether explanatory novelty, especially in physics, has actually been achieved. The world implied here is one where candidate insight is easier to generate than to validate.
